title: DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEPENING HIDDEN REPRESENTATIONS FROM - PRE TRAINED LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transformer-based pre-trained language models have proven to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. However, current approaches only take advantage of the output of the encoder’s final layer when fine-tuning the downstream tasks. We argue that only taking single layer’s output restricts the power of pre-trained representation. Thus we deepen the representation learned by the model by fusing the hidden representation in terms of an explicit HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), which automatically absorbs the complementary representation with respect to the output from the final layer. Utilizing RoBERTa as the backbone encoder, our proposed improvement over the pre-trained models is shown effective on multiple natural language understanding tasks and help our model rival with the state-of-the-art models on the GLUE benchmark.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Language representation is essential to the understanding of text. Recently, pre-trained language models based on Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) such as GPT (Radford et al., 2018), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) have been shown to be effective for learning contextualized language representation. These models have since continued to achieve new state-of-the-art results on a variety of natural language processing tasks. They include question answering (Rajpurkar et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2017), natural language inference (Williams et al., 2018; Bowman et al., 2015), named entity recognition (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003), sentiment analysis (Socher et al., 2013) and semantic textual similarity (Cer et al., 2017; Dolan & Brockett, 2005).

Normally, Transformer-based models are pre-trained on large-scale unlabeled corpus in an unsupervised manner, and then fine-tuned on the downstream tasks through introducing task-specific output layer. When fine-tuning on the supervised downstream tasks, the models pass directly the output of Transformer encoder’s final layer, which is considered as the contextualized representation of input text, to the task-specific layer.

However, due to the numerous layers (i.e., Transformer blocks) and considerable depth of these pretrained models, we argue that the output of the last layer may not always be the best representation of the input text during the fine-tuning for downstream tasks. Devlin et al. (2019) shows diverse combinations of different layers’ outputs of the pre-trained BERT result in distinct performance on CoNLL-2003 Named Entity Recognition (NER) task (Tjong Kim Sang & De Meulder, 2003). Peters et al. (2018b) points out for pre-trained language models, including Transformer, the most transferable contextualized representations of input text tend to occur in the middle layers, while the top layers specialize for language modeling. Therefore, the onefold use of the last layer’s output may restrict the power of the pre-trained representation.

In this paper, we propose an extra network component design for Transformer-based model, which is capable of adaptively leveraging the hidden information in the Transformer’s hidden layers to refine the language representation. Our introduced additional components include two main additional components:
1. HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

2. Fusion network integrates the hidden information extracted by the HIRE with Transformer final layer’s output through two steps of functionalities, leading to a refined contextualized language representation.

Taking advantage of the robustness of RoBERTa by using it as our backbone Transformer-based encoder (Liu et al., 2019c), we conduct experiments on GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2018), which consists of nine Natural Language Understanding (NLU) tasks. With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset. Keeping the backbone Transformer model unchanged on its architecture, pre-training procedure and training objectives, we get comparable performance with other state-of-the-art models on the GLUE leaderboard, which verifies the effectiveness of the proposed HIRE enhancement over Transformer model.

## 2.1 TRANSFORMER-BASED ENCODER

Transformer-based encoder is responsible for encoding input text into contextualized representation. Let _{w_ 1 _, . . . , wn}_ represent a sequence of _n_ words of input text, Transformer-based encoder encodes the input sequence into its universal contextualized representation _**R** ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ :

where _d_ is the hidden size of the encoder and _**R**_ is the output of Transformer-based encoder’s last layer which has the same length as the input text. We call it **preliminary representation** in this paper to distinguish it with the one that we introduce in section 2.2. Here, we omit a rather extensive formulations of Transformer and refer readers to Vaswani et al. (2017), Radford et al. (2018) and Devlin et al. (2019) for more details.

## 2.2 HIDDEN REPRESENTATION EXTRACTOR

Transformer-based encoder normally has many structure-identical layers stacked together, for example, BERTLARGE and XLNetLARGE all contain 24 layers of the identical structure, either outputs

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

from these hidden layers or the last layer, but not only limited to the latter, may be extremely helpful for specific downstream task. To make full use of the representations from these hidden layers, we introduce an extra component attached to the original encoder, HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE) to capture the complementary information that the output of the last layer fails to capture. Since each layer does not take the same importance to represent a certain input sequence for different downstream tasks, we design an adaptive mechanism that can compute the importance dynamically. We measure the importance by an **importance score** .

The input to the HIRE is _{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}_ where _l_ represents the number of layers in the encoder. Here _**H**_ 0 is the initial embedding of input text, which is the input of the encoder’s first layer but is updated during training and _**H** j ∈_ R _[n][×][d]_ is the hidden-state of the encoder at the output of layer _j_ . For the sake of simplicity, we call them all hidden-state afterwards.

We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together. In this way, we manage to summarize the hidden-state into a fixed-sized vector. Hence, we obtain _**U** ∈_ R[(] _[l]_[+1)] _[×]_[4] _[d]_ with _**u** i_ the summarized vector of _**H** i_ :

where 0 _≤ i ≤ l_ . Then the importance value _αi_ for hidden-state _**H** i_ is calculated by:

where _**W** ∈_ R[4] _[d][×]_[1] and _b ∈_ R are trainable parameters. Let _**α**_ = _{αi}_ be normalized into a probability distribution _**s**_ through a softmax layer:
where _si_ is the normalized weight of hidden-state _i_ when computing the representation. Subsequently, we obtain the input sequence’s new representation _**A**_ by:

With the same shape as the output of Transformer-based encoder’s final layer, HIRE’s output _**A**_ is expected to contain the additional useful information from the encoder’s hidden-states and we call it **complementary representation** .

## 2.3 FUSION NETWORK

This module fuses the information contained in the output of Transformer-based encoder and the one extracted from encoders’ hidden states by HIRE.

Given the preliminary representation _**R**_ , instead of letting it flow directly into task-specfic output layer, we combine it together with the complementary representation _**A**_ to yield _**M**_ , defined by:
where _◦_ is element-wise multiplication (Hadamard Product) and [; ] is concatenation across the last dimension.

Later, two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of _d_ for each direction, is used to fully fuse the information contained in the preliminary representation and the complementary representation. We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation:

## 2.4 OUTPUT LAYER

The output layer is task-specific. The following are the concerned implementation details on two tasks, classification and regression.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

For classification task, given the input text’s contextualized representation _**F**_ , following Devlin et al. (2019), we take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation. Let _m_ be the number of labels in the datasets, we pass _**c**_ through a feed-forward network (FFN):

with _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R[2] _[d][×][d]_ , _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][×][m]_ , _**b**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d]_ and _**b**_ 2 _∈_ R _[m]_ the only parameters that we introduce in output layer. Finally, the probability distribution of predicted label is computed as:
For regression task, we obtain _q_ in the same manner with _m_ = 1, and take _q_ as the predicted value.

## 2.5 TRAINING

For classification task, the training loss to be minimized is defined by the Cross-Entropy:
where _**θ**_ is the set of all parameters in the model, _T_ is the number of examples in the dataset, _pi,c_ is the predicted probability of gold class _c_ for example _i_ .

For regression task, we define the training loss by mean squared error (MSE):

where _qi_ is the predicted value for example _i_ and _yi_ is the ground truth value for example _i_ .

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET

We conducted the experiments on the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark (Wang et al., 2018) to evaluate our proposed method. GLUE is a collection of 9 diverse datasets[1] for training, evaluating, and analyzing natural language understanding models.

Table 1: GLUE Dev results. Our results are based on single model trained with single task and a median over five runs with different random seed but the same hyperparameter is reported for each task. The results of MT-DNN, XLNETLARGE, ALBERT and RoBERTa are from Liu et al. (2019b), Yang et al. (2019), Lan et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2019c). See the lower-most row for the performance of our approach. Mc, acc and pearson denote Matthews correlation, accuracy and Person correlation coefficient respectively.

> 1All the datasets can be obtained from https://gluebenchmark.com/tasks

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

|Model|Params|Shared|
|---|---|---|
||(M)|(M)|
|ALBERT-xxlarge|235|-|
|MT-DNN|350|340|
|RoBERTa|355|-|
|RoBERTa+HIRE|437|-|

Table 2: Parameter Comparison.

Table 1 compares our method with a list of Transformer-based models on the development set. Model parameter comparison is shown in Table 2. To obtain a direct and fair comparison with our baseline model RoBERTa, following the original paper (Liu et al., 2019c), we fine-tune RoBERTa+HIRE separately for each of the GLUE tasks, using only task-specific training data. The single-model results for each task are reported. We run our model with five different random seeds but the same hyperparameters and take the median value. Due to the problematic nature of WNLI dataset, we exclude its results in this table. The results shows that RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets, with an improvement of 1.7 points, 0.4 points, 0.5/0.2 points, 0.3 points on CoLA, SST-2, MNLI and QNLI respectively. And on the MRPC, STS-B and RTE task, our model get the same result as RoBERTa. It should be noted that the improvement is entirely attributed to the introduction of HIdden Representation Extractor and fusion network in our model.

Table 3: GLUE Test results, scored by the official evaluation server. All the results are obtained from GLUE leaderboard (https://gluebenchmark.com/leaderboard). The number below each task’s name indicates the size of training dataset. Recently the GLUE benchmark has forbidden all the submissions to treat the QNLI as a ranking task, which results in the missing of some models’ accuracies on the QNLI. _[a]_ Devlin et al. (2019); _[b]_ Liu et al. (2019b); _[c]_ Zhu et al. (2020); _[d]_ Yang et al. (2019); _[e]_ Lan et al. (2020); _[f]_ Liu et al. (2019c).

Table 3 presents the results of HIRE enhancement and other models on the test set that have been submitted to the GLUE leaderboard. Following Liu et al. (2019c), we fine-tune STS-B and MRPC starting from the MNLI single-task model. Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI, and the large-scale nature of MNLI dataset (393k), we also initialize RoBERTa+HIRE with the weights of MNLI single-task model before fine-tuning on RTE and WNLI. We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard. The results show that RoBERTa+HIRE still boosts the strong RoBERTa baseline model on the test set. To be specific, RoBERTa+HIRE outperforms RoBERTa over CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, SST-B, MNLI-mm and QNLI with an improvement of 0.8 points, 0.4 points, 0.7/0.9 points, 0.2/0.1 points, 0.2 points and 0.1 points respectively. In the meantime, RoBERTa+HIRE gets the same results as RoBERTa on QQP and WNLI. By category, RoBERTa+HIRE has better performance than RoBERTa on the single sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks. It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1. The results are quite promising since HIRE does not modify the encoder internal architecture (Yang et al., 2019) or redefine the pre-training procedure (Liu et al., 2019c) , getting the comparable results with them.

## 4 ABLATION STUDY

In this section, we perform a set of ablation experiments to understand the effects of our proposed techniques during fine-tuning. All the results reported in this section are a median of five random runs.

## 4.1 MODEL DESIGN CONSIDERATION

||CoLA|STS-B|
|---|---|---|
|Model|(Mc)|(Pearson)|
|Our model|69.7|92.4|
|w/o HIRE|68.5|91.5|
|w/o Fusion network|68.2|92.2|

Table 4: Ablation study over model design consideration on the development set of CoLA and STSB. The result for each model is a median of five random runs. Both HIRE and fusion network significantly improve the model performance on all two datasets.

To individually evaluate the importance of HIRE and fusion network, we vary our model in the following ways and conduct the experiments on the development set of CoLA and STS-B. The results are in Table 4:

- We remove the HIRE from our model and pass the preliminary representation _**R**_ directly into the fusion network. In order to keep the fusion network, we fuse the preliminary representation with itself, which means we define instead _**M**_ by:

The results are presented in row 2.

- We remove the fusion network, and take the outputs of HIRE as the final representation of the input and flow it directly into the output layer and present the results in row 3.

As can be seen from the table, to use of HIRE to extract the hidden information is crucial to the model: Matthews correlation of CoLA and Pearson correlation coefficient of STS-B drop dramatically by 1.2 and 0.9 points if it’s removed. We also observe that fusion network is an important component that contributes 1.5/0.2 gains on the CoLA and STS-B tasks. The results are aligned with our assumption that HIRE extracts the complementary information from the hidden layers while the fusion network fuses it with the preliminary representation.

We investigate how the adaptive assignment mechanism of the importance score affects the model’s performance. CoLA is chosen as the downstream task. Table 5 compares our proposed mechanism

|Number|0|1|2|3|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Mc|68.5|69.3|**69.7**|68.5|
Table 6: Ablation study over GRU layer number in fusion network on the development set of CoLA. The best result is in **bold** .

with diverse fixed combinations of importance scores over different range of layers: first 6, last 6 or all 25 layers. Two strategies are studied: _mean_ and _random_ . In the _mean_ situation, we suppose all the layers contribute exactly the same. On the contrary, under the _random_ condition, we generate a score for each layer randomly and do the SoftMax operation across all the layers.

From the table, we observe that fixing each layer’s importance score for all examples hurts the model performance. Across all rows, we find that none of single strategy can yield the best performance. The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.

## 4.3 EFFECT OF GRU LAYER NUMBER IN FUSION NETWORK

To investigate the effect of GRU layer number in the fusion network, we set the GRU layer number from 0 to 3 and conduct the ablation experiments on the development dataset of CoLA. Table 6 shows the results. We observe that the modest number 2 would be a better choice.

## 5 ANALYSIS

Figure 2: **Left** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers when computing the complementary representation for various NLU tasks. The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer. **Right** : Distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example.

We compare the importance score’s distribution of different NLU tasks. For each task, we run our best single model over the development set and the results are calculated by averaging the values across all the examples within each dataset. The results are presented in the left of Figure 2. From the top to the bottom of the heatmap, the results are placed in the following order: single-sentence tasks, similarity and paraphrase tasks, and natural language inference tasks. From it, we find that the distribution differs among the different tasks, which demonstrates HIRE’s dynamic ability to adapt for distinct tasks when computing the complimentary representation. The most important contribution occurs below the final layer for all the tasks except STS-B and RTE. All layers have a close contribution for STS-B and RTE task. In addition, we find that QNLI, a classification task which requires the model to determine whether the context sentence contains the answer to the question, relies on layers 2-4 to some extent. In fact, Jawahar et al. (2019) state that BERT mostly captures phrase-level (or span-level) information in the lower layers and that this information gets gradually diluted in higher layers. Since QNLI is derived from the span extraction question answering dataset

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

SQuAD, where the answer is a span of the input text, we speculate that HIRE takes advantage of this information when making the prediction.

The right of Figure 2 presents the distribution of importance scores over different layers for each example of SST-2 dataset. The number on the ordinate axis denotes the index of the example. It shows that HIRE can adapt not only distinct tasks but also the different examples. In the meantime, we observe also that even though there are subtle differences among these examples, they follow certain same patterns when calculating the complementary representation, for example, layers 21 and 22 contribute the most for almost all the examples and also the layers around them. But the figure shows also that for some examples, all layers contribute nearly equally.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Transformer model is empowered by self-attention mechanism and has been applied as an effective model architecture design in quite a lot of pre-trained language models (Vaswani et al., 2017). OpenAI GPT (Radford et al., 2018) is the first model that introduced Transformer architecture into unsupervised pre-training. But instead of unidirectional training like GPT, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) adopts Masked LM objective when pre-training, which enables the representation to incorporate context from both direction. The next sentence prediction (NSP) objective is also used by BERT to better model the relationship between sentences. Trained with dynamic masking, large mini-batches and a larger byte-level BPE, full-sentences without NSP, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c) improves BERT’s performance on the downstream tasks from a better BERT re-implementation training on BooksCorpus(Zhu et al., 2015), CC-News, Openwebtext and Stories. In terms of finetuning on downstream tasks, all these powerful Transformer-based models help various NLP tasks continuously achieve new state-of-the-art results. Diverse new methods have been proposed recently for fine-tuning the downstream tasks, including multi-task learning (Liu et al., 2019b), adversarial training (Zhu et al., 2020) or incorporating semantic information into language representation (Zhang et al., 2020).

Traditionally, downstream tasks or back-end part of models take representations from the last layer of the pre-trained language models as the default input. However, recent studies trigger researchers’ interest on the intermediate layers’ representation learned by pre-trained models. When studying the linguistic knowledge and transferability of contextualized word representations with a series of seventeen diverse probing tasks, Liu et al. (2019a) observe that Transformers tend to encode transferable features in their intermediate layers, aligned with the results by Peters et al. (2018b). Jawahar et al. (2019) show a rich hierarchy of linguistic information is encoded by BERT, with surface features, syntactic features and semantic features lying from bottom to top in the layers. So far, to our best knowledge, exploiting the representations learned by hidden layers is still stuck with limited empirical observations, which motivates us to propose a general solution for fully exploiting all levels of representations learned by the pre-trained language models. However, we want to note that a freezing/fine-tuning discrepancy may exist, since previous observations are made by adopting a feature extracting approach while our work is conducted in the fine-tuning scenario.

Several works have made attempts to utilize the information from the intermediate layers of deep models (Zhao et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019). Peters et al. (2018a) propose a deep contextualized word representation called ELMo, which is a task-specific linear combination of the intermediate layer representations in the bidirectional LSTM language model. Tenney et al. (2019) find that using ELMo-style scalar mixing of layer activations, both deep Transformer models (BERT and GPT) gain a significant performance improvement on a novel edge probing task. Zhu et al. (2018) adopt a linear combination of embeddings from different layers in BERT to encode tokens for conversational question answering but in a feature-based way. Compared with these works, our approach still presents the following innovations: 1) Our dynamic layer weighting mechanism calculates importance scores for each example over different layers, which is distinct from previous works (e.g., ELMo), where the contribution score (in the form of a parameter) for each layer is always same for all the examples of the same task. 2) Our approach fuses the representation weighted from the hidden layers and the one from the last layer to get a refined representation. 3) To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 7 CONCLUSION

This paper presents HIdden Representation Extractor (HIRE), a novel enhancement component that refines language representation by adaptively leveraging the Transformer-based model’s hidden layers. In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer. A lite fusion network then incorporates the outputs of HIRE into those of the original model. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of refined language representation for natural language understanding. The analysis highlights the distinct contribution of each layer’s output for diverse tasks and different examples.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: The paper proposes HIRE, an extra component that dynamically weights hidden-layer representations from a Transformer encoder and fuses them with the final-layer output to improve downstream NLU task performance. The proposal is clearly motivated and the experiments on GLUE demonstrate modest but consistent improvements over the RoBERTa baseline. However, several technical details are underspecified or inconsistent, which would hamper reproducibility. The dynamic mechanism's importance is supported by ablations, but the magnitude of the improvements and some of the claims (e.g., 'state-of-the-art on SST-2') need scrutiny, especially given the parameter overhead (437M vs. 355M, a 23% increase) which is not adequately acknowledged in the performance comparisons.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming baseline on 5/9 GLUE tasks contradicts the numbers shown
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1, RoBERTa+HIRE is reported to beat RoBERTa on CoLA (+1.7), SST-2 (+0.4), MNLI-m/mm (+0.5/+0.2), and QNLI (+0.3). That is 4 tasks (with MNLI counted as one). The paper itself later says 'RoBERTa+HIRE consistently outperforms RoBERTa on 4 of the GLUE task development sets'. The '5/9' claim in the intro is inconsistent with both Table 1 and the later '4' claim. Additionally, QQP shows a regression (-0.2). This mismatch is a real inconsistency.

comments.title: State-of-the-art claim on SST-2 not fully supported by Table 3
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows RoBERTa+HIRE achieves 97.1 on SST-2, but XLNet and ALBERT also achieve 97.1 (both in bold). Therefore the result is tied for state-of-the-art, not strictly state-of-the-art. The abstract also states 'help our model rival with the state-of-the-art', which is fairer; the claim in Section 3.2 overstates this result.

comments.title: Parameter overhead understated in comparison
comments.quote: RoBERTa+HIRE|437|-
comments.explanation: Table 2 shows RoBERTa+HIRE uses 437M parameters versus 355M for RoBERTa (a ~23% increase, i.e., ~82M additional parameters from the Bi-GRUs and fusion network). The paper attributes all improvements on GLUE 'entirely... to the introduction of HIRE and fusion network', but does not discuss whether similar gains could be obtained by simply using a comparably-sized RoBERTa variant or by adding comparable parameters in a different way. This is a significant confound for the comparison that the paper does not address.

comments.title: HIRE GRU input/output dimensions and summarization mechanism underspecified
comments.quote: We use the same 2-layer Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (Cho et al., 2014) to summarize each hidden-state of the encoder. Instead of taking the whole output of GRU as the representation of the hidden state, we concatenate GRU’s each layer and each direction’s final state together.
comments.explanation: The text states the summarized vector u_i ∈ R^{4d}, which is consistent with 2 layers × 2 directions × d hidden units = 4d only if the GRU hidden size equals d. This is never stated explicitly. Also, whether the same GRU parameters are shared across all l+1 hidden-states (the figure caption hints at this, but the text is ambiguous) needs clarification. The choice of GRU hidden size is a reproducibility-relevant detail.

comments.title: Fusion output dimension and shape inconsistency
comments.explanation: For the ablation that removes HIRE (Eq. 12), M = [R; R; R+R; R∘R]. With four concatenated n×d matrices, this should be n×4d, which matches what is written. However, the main text fusion formula (implied from context) fuses R with A of the same shape, producing M ∈ R^{n×4d}. Then 'two-layer bidirectional GRU, with the output size of d for each direction' produces the fused representation F. The text says 'We concatenate the outputs of the GRUs in two dimensions together for the final contextualized representation' — it is unclear what 'in two dimensions' means precisely (concatenating across both layers and directions? Or just two directions?). Given the output layer uses c ∈ R^{2d}, this suggests only the two directions are concatenated (yielding 2d), not the layers. The phrasing 'in two dimensions' is ambiguous and could mislead reproducers.

comments.title: Output token for classification may not be <s> after Bi-GRU
comments.quote: we take the first row _**c** ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ of _**F**_ corresponding to the first input token ( _< s >_ ) as the aggregated representation
comments.explanation: After passing the fused representation M through a 2-layer Bi-GRU, the first position's output is no longer the direct <s> token representation in the usual BERT/RoBERTa sense — it has been transformed by recurrent processing. While the claim that the first row c corresponds to the first input token is technically true (positionally), calling it 'the aggregated representation' following Devlin et al. is slightly misleading because the original BERT pooling uses the [CLS] token of the encoder's final layer, not a downstream RNN. This is a minor framing issue but could mislead readers about the role of <s> here.

comments.title: Softmax normalization details for importance scores
comments.quote: Let _**α**_ = _{αi}_ be normalized into a probability distribution _**s**_ through a softmax layer
comments.explanation: The mechanism for assigning importance scores is described, but the paper only partially specifies it. The softmax is presumably applied across the l+1 layer indices for each example (globally, not per token). This is implied but not explicitly stated. Given Section 5 discusses 'different examples' having different distributions, the scores must be computed per-example. The u_i vectors summarize each layer into a single fixed-size vector, so this is consistent, but a reader reproducing the method would want this stated explicitly.

comments.title: Ensemble details for test set submission not provided
comments.quote: We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard.
comments.explanation: Table 3 reports test-set results that are from an ensemble, while Table 1's dev results are single-model. The paper does not specify how many models were ensembled, how they were combined, or how the ensemble was constructed (seeds, hyperparameters, snapshots?). This is a substantive methodological gap that makes comparison against single-model baselines on the dev set misleading and hinders reproducibility of the leaderboard results.

comments.title: Claim about first one to exploit intermediate information during fine-tuning
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, our work is the first one that takes advantage of the pre-trained language model’s intermediate information in the context of fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: This is a strong novelty claim. Kondratyuk & Straka (2019) '75 Languages, 1 Model' used scalar mix of BERT layers during fine-tuning for parsing, and Su et al. and others have also fine-tuned with layer-combination schemes. Even Tenney et al.'s 'BERT Rediscovers the NLP Pipeline' uses scalar mixing with trainable weights. While the dynamic per-example mechanism (innovation #1) may be novel, the blanket claim of being 'the first one' to exploit intermediate information in fine-tuning overstates the contribution.

comments.title: Table 4 numbers inconsistent with prose
comments.quote: Matthews correlation of CoLA and Pearson correlation coefficient of STS-B drop dramatically by 1.2 and 0.9 points if it’s removed.
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows the full model at 69.7/92.4 and 'w/o HIRE' at 68.5/91.5. Differences are 1.2 and 0.9, which matches. For 'w/o Fusion network' at 68.2/92.2, differences are 1.5/0.2. The prose says fusion network 'contributes 1.5/0.2 gains' which matches Table 4. So the numbers are internally consistent. The characterization of a 0.2 drop as fusion being 'an important component that contributes' seems somewhat overstated given how small the STS-B effect is, but this is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Importance-score formula omits softmax temperature/scale details
comments.quote: where _**W** ∈_ R[4] _[d][×]_[1] and _b ∈_ R are trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The importance score α_i is a linear projection of u_i (presumably u_i^T W + b, producing a scalar). The formula itself is not fully shown in the extracted text, but the dimension of W is given as 4d×1 which is consistent with the fixed-size u_i ∈ R^{4d}. One concern: because u_i is already a fixed vector per layer (not per token/example), the same transformation is applied, but per-example conditioning comes through u_i being computed from each example's H_i. This is fine conceptually, but the paper could more clearly state that the importance score is a function of that example's hidden state summary — the adaptive/dynamic property relies on this but is not emphasized.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a plausible and relatively simple extension to RoBERTa that uses learned, example-dependent layer weighting plus a fusion network, and the empirical results suggest small but consistent gains on several GLUE metrics. The main technical idea is understandable and many dimensional choices appear internally consistent. The most important issues are not algebraic mistakes so much as inconsistencies between the stated interpretation and the formal method, plus reproducibility and comparison concerns: the paper sometimes describes HIRE as using hidden layers excluding the final layer even though the formal definition includes the final layer; the test-set procedure uses ensembles and MNLI-initialization without enough detail; and several claims about significance, state-of-the-art status, and dynamic adaptation are stronger than what the reported experiments directly establish.

comments.title: HIRE is described as excluding the final layer, but the formal definition includes it
comments.quote: In our proposed model design, HIRE dynamically generates complementary representation from all hidden layers other than that from the default last layer.
comments.explanation: I initially read this sentence as saying that HIRE forms its representation only from intermediate layers, excluding the usual final-layer representation, which would match the motivation that the method extracts information the last layer misses. However, in Section 2.2 the input to HIRE is defined as \(_{_ _**H**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**H** j, . . . ,_ _**H** l}\), where \(_**H**_j\) is the output of encoder layer \(j\), and \(l\) is the number of layers. Thus \(_**H**_l\), the final layer output, is included in the softmax-weighted sum used to form \(_**A**_\). Table 5 also refers to “all 25 layers,” consistent with 24 RoBERTa layers plus embeddings. The implementation may well be reasonable, but the prose claim that HIRE uses layers “other than” the default last layer contradicts the formal construction and could lead readers to implement a different variant.

comments.title: Layer index 0 is mislabeled in the analysis figure
comments.quote: The numbers on the abscissa axis indicate the corresponding layer with 0 being the first layer and 24 being the last layer.
comments.explanation: The paper earlier defines \(_**H**_0\) as the initial embedding of the input text, not the output of the first Transformer layer. For RoBERTa-large, there are 24 Transformer layers, so an index range 0–24 naturally means embedding plus layers 1–24. Table 5’s phrase “all 25 layers” also supports that interpretation. Therefore the figure caption’s statement that 0 is “the first layer” is inconsistent with the model definition. This matters for the qualitative analysis: if a bright region appears at index 0, it should be interpreted as reliance on the embedding representation, not on the first Transformer block.

comments.title: The abstract’s “5/9” improvement claim is not clearly supported by the reported tables
comments.quote: With the help of HIRE, our model outperforms the baseline on 5/9 of them and advances the state-of-the-art on SST-2 dataset.
comments.explanation: I checked this against both the development and test results. On the development set in Table 1, WNLI is excluded and the text below the table says the method outperforms RoBERTa on 4 GLUE task development sets: CoLA, SST-2, MNLI, and QNLI. On the test set in Table 3, the method improves over RoBERTa on several metrics, but depending on whether MNLI matched/mismatched and paired metrics such as MRPC/STS-B are counted as tasks or metrics, the count is not simply “5/9.” WNLI is tied and RTE is worse. The claim may have been intended as a high-level summary, but as written it is ambiguous and appears inconsistent with the more precise table-level accounting.

comments.title: State-of-the-art claim on SST-2 appears to be a tie rather than an advance
comments.quote: It is worth noting that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on SST-2 dataset, with a score of 97.1.
comments.explanation: Within Table 3, RoBERTa+HIRE obtains 97.1 on SST-2, which is indeed the best displayed value, but XLNet and ALBERT also have 97.1 in the same column. Thus the result matches the best reported score in the table rather than clearly advancing it. This is a small wording issue, but the abstract says the method “advances the state-of-the-art,” which overstates what is shown by the cited comparison unless there is additional evidence outside the table.

comments.title: Test-set ensemble procedure is underspecified and complicates comparison with the baseline
comments.quote: Following Liu et al. (2019c), we fine-tune STS-B and MRPC starting from the MNLI single-task model. Given the simplicity between RTE, WNLI and MNLI, and the large-scale nature of MNLI dataset (393k), we also initialize RoBERTa+HIRE with the weights of MNLI single-task model before fine-tuning on RTE and WNLI. We submitted the ensemble-model results to the leaderboard.
comments.explanation: The use of MNLI initialization for some tasks follows a known RoBERTa practice, so the general intent is clear. What remains underspecified is the ensemble procedure: the paper does not state how many models were ensembled, whether they differ by random seed or hyperparameters, how predictions were combined, or whether the RoBERTa baseline row is a single model or an ensemble under the same protocol. This matters because the test-set gains over RoBERTa are small on several tasks, and ensembling can easily account for tenths of a point. Without these details, the leaderboard comparison is hard to reproduce and the attribution of improvements specifically to HIRE is less secure.

comments.title: Fine-tuning and training hyperparameters needed for reproduction are missing
comments.quote: Our results are based on single model trained with single task and a median over five runs with different random seed but the same hyperparameter is reported for each task.
comments.explanation: Reporting medians over five seeds is good practice, and using the same hyperparameters across seeds is sensible. However, the paper does not provide the actual hyperparameters needed to reproduce the results: learning rate, batch size, number of epochs or update steps, warmup schedule, optimizer settings, dropout, maximum sequence length, gradient clipping, checkpoint selection criterion, or whether RoBERTa and the newly added GRUs use the same learning rate. Since the reported improvements are often small, these choices are important. A reader could implement the architecture but still be unable to reproduce the experimental protocol.

comments.title: The “complementary representation” interpretation is stronger than what the mechanism enforces
comments.quote: HIRE dynamically learns a complementary representation which contains the information that the final layer’s output fails to capture.
comments.explanation: The motivation is reasonable: intermediate layers may encode useful information not emphasized by the top layer. But the formal HIRE output is a softmax-weighted sum over layer representations, apparently including the final layer itself, with weights derived from a GRU summary. There is no objective, orthogonality constraint, residual decomposition, or diagnostic test showing that \(_**A**_\) specifically contains information absent from \(_**R**_\). It may simply be another learned mixture of representations, possibly often dominated by layers near the top. The term “complementary” is useful as an intuition, but the sentence states it as a property of the learned representation rather than a hypothesis or empirical interpretation.

comments.title: Ablation claims use “significantly” without statistical evidence
comments.quote: Both HIRE and fusion network significantly improve the model performance on all two datasets.
comments.explanation: The ablation results in Table 4 are directionally supportive: removing HIRE or removing the fusion network lowers the median score on CoLA and STS-B. However, the paper reports only medians over five runs and gives no standard deviations, confidence intervals, paired tests, or per-seed results. In GLUE fine-tuning, especially on smaller datasets such as CoLA and STS-B, run-to-run variance can be large enough that differences of a few tenths may not be statistically reliable. The qualitative conclusion that the components help is plausible, but the word “significantly” is not justified by the reported evidence.

comments.title: The dynamic weighting ablation does not fully support the claimed adaptation across diverse tasks
comments.quote: The results enable us to conclude that the hidden-state weighting mechanism introduced by HIRE may indeed adapt to diverse downstream tasks for better performance.
comments.explanation: Table 5 is a useful check that learned example-dependent weights outperform several fixed weighting schemes on CoLA. But this particular ablation is conducted only on CoLA, so it does not by itself establish adaptation to “diverse downstream tasks.” The later heatmap analysis shows that average weights differ across tasks, but it is descriptive rather than an ablation showing that dynamic weighting improves each task. The cautious word “may” helps, yet the conclusion still reaches beyond the direct evidence in this subsection.

comments.title: Random fixed-weight baseline is not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: On the contrary, under the _random_ condition, we generate a score for each layer randomly and do the SoftMax operation across all the layers.
comments.explanation: This baseline is meant to test whether the learned importance mechanism matters, which is a worthwhile comparison. However, it is unclear what distribution the random scores are sampled from, whether they are sampled once and fixed for the whole experiment, resampled per run, resampled per example, or resampled during training, and whether the same random weights are used across seeds. These choices can produce different baselines: a fixed random layer mixture is quite different from per-example random weighting. The missing details make the ablation hard to reproduce and interpret.
